items.title: Diagnostic Thresholds for Pulmonary Hypertension (PH)
items.content: The diagnostic criteria for PH have been updated to facilitate earlier detection and intervention. The mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP) threshold has been reduced from ≥25 mmHg to >20 mmHg, and the pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) level for identifying precapillary PH, including PAH, is now set at >2 Wood units (WU).
keyPoints: Mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP) diagnostic threshold for PH: >20 mmHg.
Pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) level for diagnosing precapillary PH and PAH: >2 Wood units (WU).
Earlier detection is emphasized to improve quality of life and lifespan.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · Pulmonary Hypertension

items.title: Importance of Early Diagnosis in Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension (PAH)
items.content: Early diagnosis of PAH is crucial due to the significant delay often experienced, which can extend up to 2 years. This delay impacts both quality of life and lifespan, making it essential for clinicians to recognize signs and symptoms promptly and conduct systematic evaluations in at-risk patients.
keyPoints: Substantial diagnostic delay in PAH: up to 2 years.
Impact on quality of life and lifespan if diagnosis is delayed.
Need for prompt recognition of PH signs and symptoms.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · Pulmonary Hypertension

items.title: Mechanisms Underlying Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension (PAH) Pathogenesis
items.content: PAH pathogenesis involves apoptosis resistance, cell proliferation, dysregulated metabolism, and increased oxidant stress in pulmonary vascular cells. These processes lead to hypertrophic, fibrotic, and plexogenic remodeling of distal pulmonary arterioles, reducing vascular compliance and promoting thrombosis.
keyPoints: Apoptosis resistance, cell proliferation, dysregulated metabolism, and oxidant stress are key mechanisms in PAH pathogenesis.
Remodeling includes hypertrophic, fibrotic, plexogenic changes, and extracellular matrix expansion with integrins, nonfibrillar collagens, and fibronectin.
Right ventricular dysfunction is due to increased afterload and depressed sarcomere function from chronic inflammation or autoimmune mechanisms.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Genetic Drivers of Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension
items.content: PAH is associated with germline variants in genes regulating growth, proliferation, and phenotype switching. BMPR2 gene mutations are the most common cause of hereditary PAH, though penetrance can be as low as 20%. Imbalance in TGF-β receptor signaling is a therapeutic target.
keyPoints: BMPR2 gene variants are the most common genetic cause of hereditary PAH with variable penetrance (~20%).
Sporadic BMPR2 mutations also occur in PAH patients.
TGF-β receptor signaling imbalance is a therapeutic target, as seen in trials for activin signal inhibitor therapy.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Epigenetic Factors and Endothelial-Mesenchymal Transition in PAH
items.content: PAH involves epigenetic mechanisms like DNA methylation/demethylation, with increased baseline DNA damage in endothelial cells. Endothelial-mesenchymal transition (End-MT) is linked to cellular phenotype switching and loss of BMPR2 expression.
keyPoints: Increased DNA damage and epigenetic changes are implicated in PAH pathogenesis.
End-MT, identified in ~6% of cells from explanted lungs, involves migration, smooth muscle actin gain, and cell-cell contact loss.
End-MT is associated with BMPR2 expression loss, linking genetic risk to a key endophenotype driving PAH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Targeting Molecular Pathways in PAH for Therapeutic Intervention
items.content: In pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH), several molecular pathways and genes are implicated, offering potential therapeutic targets. Overactivation of JAK2 contributes to cellular proliferation and experimental PH, which can be inhibited by ruxolitinib. HIF-2α signaling is another modifiable pathway; inhibition with PT2567 has shown improvements in hemodynamics and vascular remodeling.
keyPoints: JAK2 overactivation in PAH can be targeted with ruxolitinib to reduce cellular proliferation.
HIF-2α inhibitor PT2567 improves hemodynamics and vascular remodeling in PAH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 2) | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Metabolic Alterations and Therapeutic Targets in PAH
items.content: PAH is characterized by metabolic changes such as the Warburg effect, where pulmonary artery smooth muscle cells preferentially synthesize lactic acid even with abundant oxygen. This leads to a proliferative phenotype. Metabolic modulating therapies are of interest, including dichloroacetate which promotes apoptosis in these cells and has shown mixed clinical results.
keyPoints: The Warburg effect in PAH involves lactic acid synthesis by pulmonary artery smooth muscle cells despite oxygen availability.
Dichloroacetate inhibits pyruvate dehydrogenase kinase, promoting apoptosis in pulmonary artery smooth muscle cells.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 2) | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Recognizing Pulmonary Hypertension: Importance of Clinical Suspicion
items.content: Pulmonary hypertension (PH) is often overlooked due to nonspecific symptoms that overlap with common conditions. An index of suspicion is crucial for diagnosis, especially in patients presenting with dyspnea and fatigue.
keyPoints: PH symptoms are nonspecific and insidious, overlapping with asthma, heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, and deconditioning.
Common initial presentations include dyspnea and/or fatigue; edema, chest pain, presyncope, and syncope indicate advanced disease.
Physical examination may reveal signs of right ventricular failure in later stages.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Initial Screening for Pulmonary Hypertension: Role of Echocardiography
items.content: Echocardiography with agitated saline study is the primary screening test for PH, providing initial estimates of pulmonary artery pressure and right ventricular function.
keyPoints: Elevated estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure (>35 mmHg) supports PH diagnosis.
Notched waveform on Doppler interrogation or a hypertrophied/dilated right ventricle are indicative signs.
A normal echocardiogram may not rule out PH; one-third of patients with undetected tricuspid regurgitation have PH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Advanced Diagnostic Evaluation for Pulmonary Hypertension
items.content: Further assessment is required when echocardiography suggests PH or in cases of unexplained dyspnea. Cardiopulmonary exercise testing and right heart catheterization are critical for comprehensive evaluation.
keyPoints: Cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET) differentiates cardiac vs pulmonary limitations; pVO₂ <15 mL/kg/min is prognostic in PH.
Right heart catheterization is the gold standard for diagnosing and assessing PH severity.
Invasive hemodynamic monitoring should be contextualized with other diagnostic tests.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Role of Pulmonary Function Testing and Imaging in PH Diagnosis
items.content: Pulmonary function testing can suggest restrictive or obstructive lung diseases as causes for dyspnea or pulmonary hypertension (PH). In pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH), a reduction in the diffusing capacity of the lungs for carbon monoxide ($DL_{CO}$) is characteristic. High-resolution CT imaging helps identify enlargement of pulmonary arteries and right heart structures, distinguishing PAH from other conditions like interstitial lung disease.
keyPoints: $DL_{CO}$ reduction is classic in PAH
High-resolution CT identifies main pulmonary artery, right ventricle, atria enlargement
CT distinguishes comorbidities such as interstitial lung disease and emphysema
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Sleep Studies in PH Patients
items.content: Nocturnal desaturation is common in patients with pulmonary hypertension, even without sleep-disordered breathing. Therefore, all patients should undergo nocturnal oximetry screening to identify potential underlying issues.
keyPoints: Nocturnal desaturation occurs frequently in PH
All PH patients should have nocturnal oximetry screening
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Hemodynamic Criteria for Diagnosing Pulmonary Hypertension
items.content: Right heart catheterization is the gold standard for diagnosing PH, with specific hemodynamic criteria. An mPAP >20 mmHg confirms PH. Precapillary and postcapillary PH are distinguished by PAWP ≤15 mmHg or >15 mmHg, respectively. Isolated precapillary PH requires a PVR >2.0 WU, while isolated postcapillary PH has PVR ≤2.0 WU.
keyPoints: mPAP >20 mmHg confirms PH
Precapillary PH: PAWP ≤15 mmHg and PVR >2.0 WU
Postcapillary PH: PAWP >15 mmHg and PVR ≤2.0 WU
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)
items.title: Hemodynamic Criteria for PAH Diagnosis
items.content: The diagnosis of pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) is based on specific hemodynamic criteria measured via right heart catheterization.
keyPoints: Sustained elevation in resting mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP) >20 mmHg
Pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) >2.0 Wood units (WU)
Pulmonary artery wedge pressure (PAWP) or left ventricular end-diastolic pressure (LVEDP) ≤15 mmHg
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)

items.subtopic: PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)
items.title: Prevalence and Demographics of Idiopathic PAH
items.content: Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (IPAH) shows a distinct demographic profile with a higher prevalence in women compared to men.
keyPoints: Mean age of PAH patients is 54–68 years old
Women are affected ~3.1-fold more than men
Prognosis less favorable in men compared to women
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)

items.subtopic: PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)
items.title: Portopulmonary Hypertension and Liver Transplant Outcomes
items.content: Portopulmonary hypertension is a significant prognostic marker in liver transplant patients, influencing postoperative outcomes.
keyPoints: 2-10% of patients with portal hypertension develop portopulmonary hypertension
100% mortality reported for mPAP ≥50 mmHg in one study
PVR >3.0 WU is a strong predictor of unfavorable outcome after transplant
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)
items.title: HIV-PAH: Prognosis and Correlation with CD4+ Count
items.content: HIV-associated pulmonary arterial hypertension (HIV-PAH) is a significant cause of mortality among HIV-infected patients, with one of the least favorable prognoses across all PH subgroups. There's no direct correlation between the stage of HIV infection and PAH development. However, data suggest an inverse relationship between CD4+ count and viral load with estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure (ePASP). An ePASP >40 mmHg is linked to a 40% increase in all-cause mortality compared to uninfected individuals.
keyPoints: HIV-PAH has one of the least favorable prognoses among PH subgroups.
No correlation between HIV infection stage and PAH development.
Inverse correlation between CD4+ count/viral load and ePASP.
ePASP >40 mmHg associated with a 40% increase in all-cause mortality.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)
items.title: PH Associated with Left Heart Disease: Indications for Intervention
items.content: Pulmonary hypertension due to left heart disease, classified under WSPH group 2, involves elevated left atrial pressure leading to pulmonary venous hypertension. In conditions like mitral stenosis or regurgitation, PH indicates the need for surgical or percutaneous valve intervention. PH is common in obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and amyloid cardiomyopathy, with ~75% of patients showing elevated mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP).
keyPoints: PH due to left heart disease involves elevated left atrial pressure.
PH indicates surgical or percutaneous valve intervention in mitral stenosis/regurgitation.
~75% of amyloid cardiomyopathy patients have elevated mPAP.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)
items.title: CTEPH: Incidence and Pathophysiology
items.content: Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) develops after chronic obstruction of pulmonary arteries, with an incidence of 3-7% following a single pulmonary embolism. Notably, 25% of CTEPH patients lack a history of clinical venous thromboembolism, suggesting subclinical events or other mechanisms. Proximal vessel involvement is marked by webbing, stricture, or fibrotic occlusion, while distal arterioles remodel through narrowing or obliteration.
keyPoints: CTEPH incidence post-pulmonary embolism: 3-7%.
25% of CTEPH patients have no history of clinical venous thromboembolism.
Proximal vessel involvement in CTEPH includes webbing, stricture, or fibrotic occlusion.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)

items.subtopic: OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE
items.title: Sarcoidosis and Pulmonary Hypertension: Mechanisms and Evaluation
items.content: In sarcoidosis, pulmonary hypertension (PH) can develop due to granulomatous inflammation of the pulmonary vessels or mechanical compression by enlarged lymph nodes. Patients presenting with progressive dyspnea and PH should undergo thorough evaluation.
keyPoints: PH in sarcoidosis is primarily due to granulomatous inflammation.
Mechanical compression by enlarged lymph nodes can also cause PH.
Progressive dyspnea and PH warrant a comprehensive evaluation.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE

items.subtopic: OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE
items.title: Sickle Cell Disease: Prevalence and Etiology of Pulmonary Hypertension
items.content: Pulmonary hypertension (PH) occurs in 6–10% of patients with sickle cell disease, due to multifactorial causes such as hemolysis, hypoxemia, thromboembolism, chronically high cardiac output, and chronic liver disease.
keyPoints: PH is present in 6–10% of patients with sickle cell disease.
Etiology includes hemolysis, hypoxemia, thromboembolism, high CO, and chronic liver disease.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE

items.title: Efficacy of Prostanoids in PAH
items.content: Prostanoids, including epoprostenol and treprostinil, are critical for managing PAH due to their vasodilatory and antiproliferative effects on vascular smooth muscle. Epoprostenol, administered via continuous intravenous infusion, improves functional capacity and survival in WHO FC III and IV patients by enhancing quality of life, mPAP, PVR, 6-MWD, and reducing mortality.
keyPoints: Epoprostenol improves functional capacity and survival in PAH.
Clinical trial results show improved quality of life, mPAP, PVR, 6-MWD, and mortality with epoprostenol.
Treprostinil has a longer half-life (~4 hours) than epoprostenol (~6 minutes), allowing subcutaneous administration.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 1)

items.title: Role of Selexipag in PAH Management
items.content: Selexipag, an oral nonprostanoid IP receptor agonist with a prolonged half-life, reduces hospitalization and disease progression risk by 43% compared to placebo. It is effective for patients on background therapy with ET-1 receptor antagonists or sildenafil.
keyPoints: Selexipag reduces the risk of hospitalization and disease progression by 43% (p < .0001).
No significant differences in mortality between selexipag and placebo groups.
Trial enrolled 1156 patients, treated for a median of 1.4 years.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Endothelin Receptor Antagonists in PAH
items.content: Endothelin receptor antagonists, such as bosentan and macitentan, inhibit ET-1's vasoconstrictive effects. Bosentan improves hemodynamics and exercise capacity, while macitentan reduces the risk of clinical worsening by 45% over 85 weeks.
keyPoints: Bosentan improves symptoms, 6-MWD, and WHO FC in PAH patients.
Macitentan decreases hazard ratio for PAH-related clinical worsening by 45%.
ERAs target ET-1's vasoconstrictive effects, improving hemodynamics and exercise capacity.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 1)

items.title: Role and Mechanism of PDE5 Inhibitors in PAH
items.content: PDE5 inhibitors, such as sildenafil and tadalafil, enhance NO-dependent vasodilation by preventing the hydrolysis of cGMP. This results in improved hemodynamics and exercise capacity (6-MWD) in patients with PAH.
keyPoints: PDE5 inhibitors prevent hydrolysis of cGMP to maximize NO-dependent vasodilation.
Sildenafil and tadalafil improve hemodynamics and 6-MWD in PAH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 2)

items.title: Efficacy of Sotatercept in PAH
items.content: Sotatercept, an activin signal inhibitor, reduces PVR and improves exercise capacity by rebalancing the TGF-β-BMPR2 pathway. It shows significant benefits in patients with moderate to severe symptoms despite existing treatments.
keyPoints: Sotatercept decreases PVR by -1.8 WU (0.3 mg/kg) and 3.0 WU (0.7 mg/kg) at week 24.
Improves 6-MWD by +40 m compared to -1.4 m for placebo.
Associated with WHO FC improvement, reduction in NT-proBNP levels, and shorter time to death or PAH worsening.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Early Combination Therapy in Incident PAH
items.content: Early, aggressive combination therapy with ambrisentan and tadalafil significantly reduces the risk of clinical worsening in incident PAH patients compared to monotherapy.
keyPoints: Combination therapy associated with a 50% lower risk of clinical worsening (death, lung transplantation, hospitalization for PAH worsening, worsening PAH).
Benefit primarily due to delayed time to first hospitalization.
No increase in adverse events noted with initial combination therapy.
Registry data supports better outcomes with dual therapy using PDE5 inhibitor plus ERA combinations.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · APPROACH TO PAH TREATMENT

items.title: Considerations for Up-Front Triple Therapy in PAH
items.content: Up-front triple combination therapy is being explored, but patient selection must consider potential cumulative off-target effects and hemodynamic challenges.
keyPoints: Clinical studies on triple therapy are mixed due to patient selection challenges.
Balance between cumulative off-target effects and need for aggressive treatment in advanced PAH.
Treatment decisions should be made with a PAH clinical center of excellence.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · APPROACH TO PAH TREATMENT

items.title: Preferred Treatment Strategies for CTEPH
items.content: Pulmonary endarterectomy is preferred for eligible patients, while riociguat or balloon pulmonary angioplasty are alternatives for those ineligible or with incomplete results.
keyPoints: Pulmonary endarterectomy is preferred for favorable anatomy and profile.
High-risk findings include distal fibrothrombotic remodeling, PVR >12 WU, right heart failure, NYHA FC IV.

Riociguat therapy or balloon pulmonary angioplasty (BPA) are alternatives; BPA reduces mPAP by ~9.3 mmHg but requires multiple procedures and has potential complications like hemoptysis and reperfusion pulmonary edema.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · APPROACH TO PAH TREATMENT

items.subtopic: UNMET AND FUTURE RESEARCH NEEDS IN PULMONARY HYPERTENSION
items.title: Importance of Early Diagnosis and Referral in PAH
items.content: Delayed diagnosis significantly impacts outcomes in pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH). Timely recognition by clinicians can improve therapeutic response and survival rates. Patients at risk should be referred early to specialty centers for advanced, multidisciplinary care.
keyPoints: Delayed diagnosis is a major barrier to improving outcome in PAH.
Timely diagnosis affects therapy response and survival.
Early referral to specialty centers ensures access to state-of-the-art care.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · UNMET AND FUTURE RESEARCH NEEDS IN PULMONARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: UNMET AND FUTURE RESEARCH NEEDS IN PULMONARY HYPERTENSION
items.title: Research Gaps in Drug Efficacy and Health Equity in PAH
items.content: The efficacy of current drugs for early-stage PAH is not well understood, necessitating further research. The approval of sotatercept marks progress with a novel drug class, but clinical trials lack diverse patient subgroup data, highlighting ongoing health inequities.
keyPoints: Role of available drugs in early-stage PAH requires investigation.
Sotatercept's approval introduces a new drug class after nearly two decades.
Clinical trial data lacks diversity, indicating health inequity issues.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · UNMET AND FUTURE RESEARCH NEEDS IN PULMONARY HYPERTENSION

items.question: What is the current diagnostic threshold for mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP) to diagnose pulmonary hypertension?
items.explanation: The diagnostic threshold for mPAP has been lowered to >20 mmHg.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · Pulmonary Hypertension

items.question: Which subtype of pulmonary hypertension is characterized by an obliterative arteriopathy and symptoms such as dyspnea, chest pain, and syncope?
items.explanation: Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) is characterized by an obliterative arteriopathy and symptoms such as dyspnea, chest pain, and syncope.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · Pulmonary Hypertension

items.question: What pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) level is now used to identify patients with PAH and other forms of precapillary PH?
items.explanation: The PVR level used to identify patients with PAH and other forms of precapillary PH has been lowered to >2 Wood units.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · Pulmonary Hypertension

items.question: Which genetic variant is the most common cause of hereditary pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH)?
items.explanation: A variant in the gene encoding bone morphogenetic protein receptor-2 (BMPR2) is the most common cause of hereditary PAH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: What cellular process contributes to hypertrophic, fibrotic, and plexogenic remodeling in pulmonary arterioles in PAH?
items.explanation: Apoptosis resistance, among other factors, contributes to the remodeling of pulmonary arterioles in PAH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which epigenetic mechanism is implicated in the pathogenesis of PAH?
items.explanation: Epigenetic disease mechanisms involving methylation and demethylation are implicated in PAH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which factor is linked to endothelial-mesenchymal transition (End-MT) in pulmonary arterial remodeling?
items.explanation: End-MT is related to a loss of BMPR2 expression in PAH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which signaling pathway is inhibited by ruxolitinib in pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH)?
items.explanation: Ruxolitinib inhibits the overactivation of Janus kinase 2 (JAK2) in PAH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 2) | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: What effect does dichloroacetate have on pulmonary artery smooth muscle cells?
options: Normalizes voltage-gated K⁺ channels and promotes apoptosis
items.explanation: Dichloroacetate inhibits pyruvate dehydrogenase kinase, normalizing voltage-gated K⁺ channels and promoting apoptosis in pulmonary artery smooth muscle cells.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 2) | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: What is the role of MMP-8 in PAH?
options: Stabilizes FAK-YAP/TAZ and suppresses extracellular matrix expansion
items.explanation: MMP-8 stabilizes mechanosensitive focal adhesion kinase-yes-associated protein/transcriptional coactivator with PDZ-binding motif (FAK-YAP/TAZ), suppressing extracellular matrix expansion and vascular fibrosis.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 2) | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: Which symptom is less common and associated with more advanced pulmonary hypertension?
items.explanation: Edema is a less common symptom and indicates more advanced disease.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: What physical examination finding may indicate right ventricular failure in pulmonary hypertension?
items.explanation: Elevated jugular venous pressure is a sign of right ventricular failure.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which test is the most important initial screening for pulmonary hypertension?
items.explanation: Echocardiography with agitated saline (bubble) study is the most important initial screening test.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: What parameter on cardiopulmonary exercise testing is prognostic in PH patients when <15 mL/kg/min?
items.explanation: Peak volume of oxygen consumption (pVO₂) is prognostic in PH patients when <15 mL/kg/min.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging finding on high-resolution CT suggests pulmonary venous disease in the absence of left heart disease?
options: Enlargement of the main pulmonary artery
Centrilobular ground glass infiltrate and thickened septa
items.explanation: In the absence of left heart disease, centrilobular ground glass infiltrate and thickened septa suggest pulmonary venous disease.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary test used to screen and diagnose chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH)?
items.explanation: Ventilation-perfusion (VQ) scanning is the primary test used to screen and diagnose CTEPH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.question: Which laboratory tests are important for screening rheumatologic diseases associated with pulmonary hypertension?
options: Antinuclear antibodies, rheumatoid factor, anti-Scl-70 antibodies
items.explanation: Screening for antinuclear antibodies, rheumatoid factor, and anti-Scl-70 antibodies is important for rheumatologic diseases associated with PH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.question: What hemodynamic criteria are used to distinguish precapillary from postcapillary pulmonary hypertension?
items.explanation: Precapillary PH is distinguished by mPAP >20 mmHg and PAWP ≤15 mmHg.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is a hemodynamic criterion for diagnosing pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH)?
options: Sustained elevation in resting mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP) >20 mmHg
Pulmonary artery wedge pressure (PAWP) or left ventricular end-diastolic pressure (LVEDP) >15 mmHg
items.explanation: The hemodynamic criteria for PAH include sustained elevation in resting mPAP >20 mmHg.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)

items.subtopic: PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)
items.question: Which group of patients is associated with a higher risk and more severe cardiopulmonary hemodynamics at diagnosis compared to idiopathic PAH?
options: Patients with hereditary PAH from a BMPR2 mutation
Patients with connective tissue disease-associated PAH
Patients with congenital heart disease-associated PAH
items.explanation: Patients with hereditary PAH from a BMPR2 mutation tend to be younger at diagnosis with more severe cardiopulmonary hemodynamics.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)

items.subtopic: PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)
items.question: What is the prevalence of idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (IPAH) in patients over the age of 60 according to the original National Institutes of Health registry?
items.explanation: Only 9% of patients with IPAH were over the age of 60 in the original NIH registry.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)

items.subtopic: PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)
items.question: In which condition is portopulmonary hypertension an established marker of adverse outcome post-liver transplant?
items.explanation: Portopulmonary hypertension is an established marker of adverse outcome in the post-liver transplant period.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)
items.question: Which pulmonary hypertension subgroup includes patients with elevated left atrial pressure due to conditions like mitral stenosis or regurgitation?
options: Pulmonary Hypertension Associated with Left Heart Disease
Pulmonary Hypertension Associated with Lung Disease
Pulmonary Hypertension Associated with Chronic Thromboembolic Disease
items.explanation: Patients with elevated left atrial pressure due to mitral stenosis or regurgitation fall under Pulmonary Hypertension Associated with Left Heart Disease.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)
items.question: What is the estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure (ePASP) threshold associated with a 40% increase in all-cause mortality for HIV-infected patients?
items.explanation: An ePASP >40 mmHg in HIV-infected patients is associated with a 40% increase in all-cause mortality.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)
items.question: In which pulmonary hypertension subgroup is PH common in obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and associated with fibroproliferative remodeling?
options: Pulmonary Hypertension Associated with Left Heart Disease
Pulmonary Hypertension Associated with Lung Disease
Pulmonary Hypertension Associated with Chronic Thromboembolic Disease
items.explanation: PH is common in obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy within the Pulmonary Hypertension Associated with Left Heart Disease subgroup.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)
items.question: What percentage of COPD patients have a mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP) greater than 20 mmHg?
items.explanation: 90% of COPD patients have an mPAP >20 mmHg.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)

items.subtopic: OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE
items.question: Which mechanism is primarily responsible for the development of pulmonary hypertension (PH) in sarcoidosis?
options: Granulomatous inflammation of the pulmonary vessels
Mechanical compression by enlarged lymph nodes
items.explanation: In sarcoidosis, PH develops mainly due to granulomatous inflammation of the pulmonary vessels.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE

items.subtopic: OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE
items.question: What percentage of patients with sickle cell disease develop pulmonary hypertension (PH)?
items.explanation: Pulmonary hypertension occurs in 6–10% of patients with sickle cell disease.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE

items.subtopic: OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE
items.question: Which condition is among the most common causes of pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) worldwide?
items.explanation: Schistosomiasis is among the most common causes of PAH worldwide.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE

items.question: Which of the following statements is true regarding epoprostenol's administration in PAH?
options: Epoprostenol is delivered as a continuous intravenous infusion.
Epoprostenol has a half-life of approximately 4 hours.
Epoprostenol is primarily used for patients in WHO Functional Class I.
items.explanation: Epoprostenol improves functional capacity and survival when delivered as a continuous intravenous infusion.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary advantage of inhaled prostacyclin therapy over infused prostacyclin therapy?
options: Inhaled prostacyclin has a longer half-life.
Inhaled prostacyclin avoids the use of infusion catheters and their associated risks.
Inhaled prostacyclin can be administered orally.
Inhaled prostacyclin is more effective in reducing mortality.
items.explanation: Inhaled prostacyclin provides benefits without the inconvenience and side effects of infusion catheters, such as infection risk.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 1)

items.question: Which statement best describes selexipag's efficacy in treating PAH?
options: Selexipag significantly reduces mortality compared to placebo.
Selexipag is only effective when used without other therapies.
Selexipag reduced the risk of hospitalization and disease progression by 43%.
Selexipag is a selective ET_A receptor antagonist.
items.explanation: Selexipag was shown to reduce the risk of hospitalization and disease progression by 43% compared to placebo.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 1)

items.question: What is a key characteristic of bosentan in treating PAH?
options: Bosentan is a selective ET_A receptor antagonist.
Bosentan has been shown to improve symptoms and exercise capacity.
Bosentan is primarily used for patients with mild heart failure symptoms.
Bosentan's efficacy was only demonstrated in phase 1 trials.
items.explanation: Bosentan, a nonselective ET_A/B receptor antagonist, has been shown to improve hemodynamics and exercise capacity.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 1)

items.question: Which mechanism is primarily responsible for the vasodilatory effect of nitric oxide pathway effectors in pulmonary arterial hypertension?
options: Generation and action of cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP)
Direct stimulation of endothelial nitric oxide synthase
items.explanation: The vasodilatory effect is primarily due to the generation and action of cGMP, which induces relaxation of arterial smooth muscle cells.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 2)

items.question: Which statement best describes the role of PDE5 inhibitors in the treatment of pulmonary arterial hypertension?
options: They increase NO production in endothelial cells.
They prevent hydrolysis of cGMP to enhance vasodilation.
They directly stimulate soluble guanylyl cyclase.
They inhibit the action of activin receptor type II.
items.explanation: PDE5 inhibitors prevent the inactivation of cGMP, thereby maximizing NO-dependent vasodilation.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 2)

items.question: What is a key effect of riociguat in patients with pulmonary arterial hypertension?
options: It inhibits the production of NO.
It stabilizes the interaction between NO and sGC, increasing cGMP levels.
It blocks activin receptor II-ALK 4/5/7 signal transduction.
It directly stimulates endothelial nitric oxide synthase.
items.explanation: Riociguat increases bioactive cGMP by stabilizing the interaction between NO and sGC, enhancing vasodilation.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 2)

items.question: Which combination therapy was shown to reduce the risk of clinical worsening in treatment-naïve, incident PAH patients according to the AMBITION trial?
items.explanation: The AMBITION trial demonstrated that combination therapy with ambrisentan and tadalafil reduced the risk of clinical worsening.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · APPROACH TO PAH TREATMENT

items.question: What is a key consideration in deciding treatment escalation, deescalation, or class switching for patients with established PAH?
options: Partnership with a PAH clinical center of excellence
items.explanation: Decisions on treatment changes should be made in partnership with a PAH clinical center of excellence.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · APPROACH TO PAH TREATMENT

items.question: What is the preferred therapy for patients with CTEPH who have favorable anatomy and profile?
items.explanation: Pulmonary endarterectomy is the preferred therapy for CTEPH patients with favorable anatomy and profile.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · APPROACH TO PAH TREATMENT

items.question: Which treatment modality for PH due to lung disease showed improvement in 6-MWD by approximately 33 meters in a clinical trial?
items.explanation: Inhaled iloprost improved the 6-MWD by approximately 33 meters in a clinical trial for patients with interstitial lung disease and PH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · APPROACH TO PAH TREATMENT

items.subtopic: UNMET AND FUTURE RESEARCH NEEDS IN PULMONARY HYPERTENSION
items.question: What is identified as a major barrier to improving outcomes in PAH?
items.explanation: Delayed diagnosis is mentioned as a major barrier.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · UNMET AND FUTURE RESEARCH NEEDS IN PULMONARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: UNMET AND FUTURE RESEARCH NEEDS IN PULMONARY HYPERTENSION
items.question: What could lead to more timely diagnosis and affect the response to therapy in PAH?
options: Improved awareness among clinicians and patients
items.explanation: Improved awareness among clinicians and patients could lead to more timely diagnosis.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · UNMET AND FUTURE RESEARCH NEEDS IN PULMONARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: UNMET AND FUTURE RESEARCH NEEDS IN PULMONARY HYPERTENSION
items.question: What is the role of currently available drugs in early-stage PAH?
options: They are well understood and effective.
They are only used in late-stage disease.
items.explanation: The role of currently available drugs in early-stage disease is not known and requires further investigation.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · UNMET AND FUTURE RESEARCH NEEDS IN PULMONARY HYPERTENSION

items.statement: A significant delay in the diagnosis of pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) can have important implications for both quality of life and lifespan.
items.explanation: A substantial delay in PAH diagnosis is common and has important implications for quality of life and lifespan.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · Pulmonary Hypertension

items.statement: The primary causes of pulmonary hypertension are right heart disease or secondary lung disease.
items.explanation: The most common causes of PH are left heart or primary lung disease, not right heart disease.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · Pulmonary Hypertension

items.statement: Genetic predisposition to pulmonary hypertension in the setting of left heart disease has been fully resolved.
items.explanation: The genetic predisposition to PH in the context of left heart disease remains unresolved.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: Hypoxia is a well-studied epigenetic driver linked to pulmonary hypertension.
items.explanation: Hypoxia is indeed the most well-studied epigenetic driver linked to PH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: In PAH, the Warburg effect leads to a non-proliferative cellular phenotype.
items.explanation: The Warburg effect in PAH results in a proliferative cellular phenotype.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 2) | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.statement: Right ventricular–pulmonary arterial uncoupling occurs due to increased myocardial energy for optimal blood perfusion.
items.explanation: Uncoupling depletes myocardial energy normally reserved for maintaining optimal blood perfusion.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 2) | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.statement: A normal echocardiogram always rules out the need for further evaluation of pulmonary hypertension.
items.explanation: A high-quality normal echocardiogram may obviate further PH evaluation, but not if tricuspid regurgitation is absent.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: Right heart catheterization is the gold standard for diagnosing pulmonary hypertension.
items.explanation: Invasive hemodynamic monitoring with right heart catheterization is indeed the gold standard.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: Nocturnal oximetry screening should be performed on all patients with pulmonary hypertension, regardless of symptoms.
items.explanation: All patients with PH should undergo nocturnal oximetry screening.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.statement: Digital subtraction pulmonary angiography is the primary test for diagnosing CTEPH.
items.explanation: Ventilation-perfusion scanning, not digital subtraction pulmonary angiography, is the primary test used to diagnose CTEPH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)
items.statement: The mean age of PAH patients is reported to be between 36 and 50 years old.
items.explanation: Contemporary data suggest the mean age of PAH patients is 54–68 years.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)

items.subtopic: PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)
items.statement: Portopulmonary hypertension and hepatopulmonary syndrome are pathophysiologically distinct.
items.explanation: Portopulmonary hypertension involves pulmonary vascular remodeling, while hepatopulmonary syndrome is characterized by abnormal vasodilation leading to intrapulmonary shunting.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)
items.statement: There is a positive correlation between the stage of HIV infection and the development of PAH.
items.explanation: There is no correlation between the stage of HIV infection and the development of PAH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)
items.statement: Approximately 25% of patients with CTEPH have a history of clinical venous thromboembolism.
items.explanation: 25% of patients with CTEPH have no history of clinical venous thromboembolism.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)

items.subtopic: OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE
items.statement: Splenectomy is a risk factor for pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) even in the absence of schistosomiasis.
items.explanation: Splenectomy is a risk factor for PAH due to impaired platelet sequestration and associated microthrombosis.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE

items.subtopic: OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE
items.statement: The efficacy of therapies directed toward pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) in schistosomiasis patients is well-established.
items.explanation: The efficacy of therapies for PAH in schistosomiasis patients is unknown.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE

items.statement: Selexipag is a prostanoid analogue used for treating PAH.
items.explanation: Selexipag is an oral nonprostanoid diphenylpyrazine derivative that acts as a prostacyclin receptor agonist.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 1)

items.statement: Ambrisentan has been shown to improve exercise tolerance and WHO FC in patients with PAH.
items.explanation: The ARIES-1 trial demonstrated that ambrisentan improves exercise tolerance, WHO FC, hemodynamics, and quality of life.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 1)

items.statement: Sildenafil and tadalafil are both PDE5 inhibitors used to treat erectile dysfunction.
items.explanation: Both sildenafil and tadalafil inhibit PDE5, which is effective in treating both pulmonary arterial hypertension and erectile dysfunction.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 2)

items.statement: Sotatercept therapy resulted in a decrease of 6-MWD compared to placebo.
items.explanation: Sotatercept improved 6-MWD by an average of +40 m, whereas the placebo group saw a decrease.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 2)

items.statement: Initial combination therapy of ambrisentan and tadalafil was associated with an increase in adverse events.
items.explanation: The AMBITION trial found that initial combination therapy was not associated with an increase in adverse events.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · APPROACH TO PAH TREATMENT

items.statement: Pulmonary endarterectomy is suitable for all patients with CTEPH regardless of their risk profile.
items.explanation: Pulmonary endarterectomy is preferred only in patients with favorable anatomy and profile, not universally.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · APPROACH TO PAH TREATMENT

items.subtopic: UNMET AND FUTURE RESEARCH NEEDS IN PULMONARY HYPERTENSION
items.statement: Health inequity in PH has been completely addressed according to clinical trial data.
items.explanation: Clinical trial data continue to lack information from diverse patient subgroups, indicating health inequity remains.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · UNMET AND FUTURE RESEARCH NEEDS IN PULMONARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: UNMET AND FUTURE RESEARCH NEEDS IN PULMONARY HYPERTENSION
items.statement: The approval of sotatercept is anticipated and represents a new drug class for PAH.
items.explanation: The anticipated approval of sotatercept paves a path toward the availability of the first novel drug class in PAH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · UNMET AND FUTURE RESEARCH NEEDS IN PULMONARY HYPERTENSION

items.assertion: Endothelial-mesenchymal transition contributes to pulmonary arterial remodeling in PAH.
items.reason: Specific markers for endothelial-mesenchymal transition are identified in a significant percentage of cells from affected patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Right ventricular dysfunction in PAH is solely due to increased right ventricular afterload.
items.reason: Chronic inflammation or autoimmune mechanisms also contribute to depressed right ventricular sarcomere function.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because right ventricular dysfunction occurs not only due to increased afterload but also from other factors.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PATHOBIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Clubbing may be seen in some chronic lung diseases concurrent with pulmonary hypertension.
items.reason: Clubbing is a sign of chronic hypoxia which can occur in chronic lung diseases associated with PH.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: A patient with a normal CPET always requires further invasive testing for PH.
items.reason: Normal CPET results often mean no further invasive testing is necessary unless there's decreased exercise tolerance from baseline.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; normal CPET does not always require further invasive testing.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Isolated precapillary PH requires a PVR >2.0 WU.
items.reason: Pulmonary vascular resistance greater than 2.0 Wood Units is necessary to diagnose isolated precapillary pulmonary hypertension.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.assertion: Patients with combined pre- and postcapillary PH have a PVR ≤2.0 WU.
items.reason: Combined pre- and postcapillary PH is defined by elevated mPAP >20 mmHg, PVR >2.0 WU, and PAWP >15 mmHg.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; combined pre- and postcapillary PH requires a PVR >2.0 WU.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)
items.assertion: PAH in the setting of congenital heart disease may be associated with a favorable prognosis if surgically corrected.
items.reason: Surgical correction can prevent the development of Eisenmenger's syndrome, which is associated with progressive pulmonary vascular remodeling and adverse outcomes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)

items.subtopic: PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)
items.assertion: PAH is more common in men than women.
items.reason: The prevalence of IPAH favors women to men by ~3.1-fold.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because PAH is more prevalent in women, as stated in the reason.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PULMONARY HYPERTENSION CLASSIFICATION | TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)
items.assertion: In amyloid cardiomyopathy, approximately 75% of patients have elevated mPAP.
items.reason: Combined pre-/postcapillary PH is the most common hemodynamic subgroup in these patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)
items.assertion: Sleep-disordered syndromes generally result in severe PH.
items.reason: PH is usually modest when associated with chronic lung disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; sleep-disordered syndromes generally result in mild PH.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · TABLE 294-1 World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension Clinical Classification (part 2)

items.subtopic: OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE
items.assertion: Schistosomiasis can lead to pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH).
items.reason: Inflammation from schistosomiasis infection induces pulmonary vascular injury through mechanisms like luminal obstruction by worm eggs and endothelial cell inflammation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · OTHER DISORDERS AFFECTING THE PULMONARY VASCULATURE

items.assertion: Treprostinil has a longer half-life than epoprostenol.
items.reason: This allows for subcutaneous administration of treprostinil.
items.explanation: Treprostinil's longer half-life (~4 hours) compared to epoprostenol (~6 minutes) facilitates its subcutaneous administration.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 1)

items.assertion: Macitentan is a selective ET_A receptor antagonist.
items.reason: Macitentan has optimized receptor binding affinity for ET_A/B receptors.
items.explanation: Macitentan is an ET_A/B receptor antagonist, not a selective ET_A receptor antagonist.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF PAH (part 1)

items.assertion: Up-front triple combination therapy for PAH is widely accepted as the standard of care.
items.reason: Clinical studies on up-front triple therapy remain mixed due to challenges in patient selection and potential off-target effects.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; up-front triple therapy is not yet a standard of care, as indicated by the mixed results from clinical studies.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · APPROACH TO PAH TREATMENT

items.assertion: Inhaled iloprost has been proven effective for all lung-PH phenotypes.
items.reason: A study focusing on COPD-PH was terminated early, presumably due to lack of benefit or adverse effects.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; inhaled iloprost's generalizability to other lung-PH phenotypes is unclear.
items.reference: Pulmonary Hypertension · APPROACH TO PAH TREATMENT
